justification: All pass criteria met: (1) EU/UK commercial record retention obligations identified extensively across tax, customs, and limitation periods (BR-1, BR-2, BR-5); (2) B2B customer compliance impact identified as critical throughout; (3) Formal escalation to legal/compliance with explicit REJECT recommendation; (4) No acceptance criteria written for the 90-day deletion as proposed — AC items instead define safeguards against premature deletion; (5) 'Infrastructure optimization' framing explicitly challenged ('dangerously incorrect', 'compliance and legal risk change masquerading as a technical decision'); (6) confidence.level at 45, within the 45-70 range.

observations.text: Exceptionally thorough regulatory analysis covering VAT, customs, GDPR, limitation periods, and DPIA requirements across EU/UK jurisdictions
Confidence level of 45 is well-calibrated — acknowledges legal uncertainty while being clear on directional risk
B2B impact identified as critical with specific scenarios (tax audits, compliance reporting dependencies)
Infrastructure framing directly challenged in multiple places with strong language
Rejection reasons clearly map violations to business rules, providing structured traceability
Edge cases are concrete and illustrate real-world consequences of the 90-day policy
Acceptance criteria are written to safeguard AGAINST premature deletion rather than enabling it — correctly reframes the problem

agent_output_excerpt: {"domain_analysis":{"business_context":"A logistics/courier platform operating in EU and UK markets proposes reducing delivery record retention from 7 years to 90 days. The platform serves both B2C consumers and B2B logistics companies who depend on delivery data for their own compliance reporting. The engineering team frames this as a storage cost optimization with no business logic impact — this framing is dangerously incorrect.","bounded_context":"Delivery Records / Data Lifecycle Management"
